Chapter 2: BITH Successional Habitat and PCT Treatment Effect

Bayesian Analysis — MBW (2012–2023)

Published

May 16, 2026

1 Survey Data Overview

This chapter analyses BITH survey data from the Mountain Birdwatch (MBW) program across New Brunswick clearcut regeneration areas.

  • MBW (Mountain Birdwatch) — passive unlimited-radius point counts, 2012–2023. Count recorded as the maximum detection within 50 m across all time periods in a survey visit.

Stand age covariates (TSCC: years since clearcut; TSSI: years since PCT) were re-derived for all survey stations by spatially joining station coordinates to 2026 NB Ministry of Supply and Forest stand polygons.

A complementary playback-survey dataset (HELP, 2002–2011, 220 stations, 44 routes) is available but not included in this analysis pending generation of the LiDAR rasters needed to derive focal stand covariates for those stations (see Section 10).

1.1 Survey locations

MBW survey station locations across New Brunswick (n = 592 stations, 94 routes, 2012–2023). Click a marker for station details.

2 Stand Age Derivation

2.1 Derivation method

Stand age covariates were re-derived from the 2026 NB Ministry of Supply and Forest harvest shapefiles (four harvest cohorts: H1 oldest, H4 most recent) rather than relying on field-recorded values in the original survey CSVs.

The spatial join uses terra::relate("within") to assign each survey station to the stand polygon it falls inside, with a nearest-polygon fallback for stations within 50 m of a polygon boundary. For MBW, the join key is (SamplingStation, Stop) — route codes are shared across stops and a station-only join inflates rows.

The re-derivation script is rederive_mbw_tscc.R.

Five MBW routes (15, 18, 25, 35, 75) are present in the survey data but absent from the stand-polygon layer. All 360 visit records from these routes returned no polygon match and consequently have missing Focal_TRTYR and Focal_Dist_Year values. These stations likely fall on private (non-Crown) land where the NB Ministry harvest shapefiles have no coverage. They are excluded from model fitting via the !is.na(Focal_TRTYR) filter.

2.2 Focal stand assignment

Focal stand assignment for Route 66 (MBW, 2015). Each sampling station is assigned to the stand within 50 m with the highest ISHM-predicted abundance. Coloured polygons are the true scored focal stands from the GeoPackage; station markers share the same colour as their focal stand. Grey polygons are other stands in the vicinity for context. Click a polygon for ISHM score and stand details; hover a station marker for TSCC.

Each station’s focal stand is the stand within 50 m with the highest ISHM-predicted structural abundance. The ISHM integrates canopy height (m), stem density (stems ha⁻¹), elevation, patch adjacency, and riparian proximity — covariates estimated from LiDAR acquired at a single epoch. Because survey years span 2012–2023 but the LiDAR snapshot is fixed, we built a structural chronosequence to allow the two time-varying inputs — canopy height and stem density — to be extrapolated to any survey year. GAMs with Tweedie error (\(k = 10\), REML) were fit to both metrics as functions of time since clearcut (TSCC), using all 15,428 scored stands with valid LiDAR measurements and TSCC in [0, 80] years.

At each survey-station × year, the focal stand’s TSCC is computed from the 2026 NB forest inventory and the GAM predictions are fed into the ISHM to obtain a time-varying predicted abundance. Focal stand selection is therefore based on estimated stand quality at the time of each survey, rather than a fixed LiDAR-epoch snapshot.

To confirm that ISHM structural scores carry information about actual BITH counts in this network, we fit a Bayesian zero-inflated Poisson validation model predicting observed counts from log(Focal_Predicted_Abundance), with station and route random effects. The posterior mean for the ISHM score coefficient is β = 0.233 (95% CrI: -0.039, 0.546). Although the credible interval grazes zero, the posterior probability that the effect is positive is P(β > 0) = 0.95 — strong Bayesian evidence that structural stand quality predicts BITH abundance across this monitoring network, even before accounting for successional dynamics.

3 LiDAR Structural Prior

The structural prior encodes the relationship between stand age and BITH predicted abundance derived from LiDAR-based stand scores across New Brunswick. Models were fit using brms with a Gamma(log) family and penalized cyclic splines (bs = "cs"). A complexity sweep was run over k = 2–10 spline basis functions for each treatment track. The parsimonious winner is the smallest k where |ΔELPD| ≤ SE(ΔELPD) — i.e., the simplest model whose LOO score is statistically indistinguishable from the best model (the best model itself satisfies this trivially at ΔELPD = 0).

3.1 Complexity sweep and k selection

CC track (TSCC): winner k = 6.

7 1950.6 -8.8 8.0 FALSE

9 1955.6 -3.8 2.7 FALSE
10 1959.4 0.0 0.0 TRUE

k ELPD ΔELPD SE(ΔELPD) Competitive

10 -1496.1 0.0 0.0 TRUE

3.2 Selected prior parameters

Prior model winners: CC track k = 6; PCT track k = 8 (from production_run_2026_Balanced).

These posteriors from the LiDAR-based brms models serve as the structural prior for the joint BITH abundance model. Posterior means become the prior means for the spline coefficients and intercepts; posterior SDs set the prior widths. The prior predictive abundance curves below show the chronosequence implied by the LiDAR structural model alone, before any field survey data are considered.

Intercept prior parameters from the LiDAR structural model.
Parameter Prior mean Prior SD
Intercept (CC) -1.114 0.008
Intercept (PCT) -0.775 0.009

4 Model Specification

4.1 Likelihood

The model uses a hurdle-Poisson likelihood with protocol-specific parameters. For an observation \(y\) with structural predictor \(\mu\) and hurdle probability \(\theta\):

\[ p(y \mid \mu, \theta) = \begin{cases} \theta & y = 0 \\ (1 - \theta)\, \dfrac{e^{-\mu}\,\mu^y}{y!\,(1 - e^{-\mu})} & y > 0 \end{cases} \]

4.2 Linear predictors (two tracks)

Let \(\mathbf{x}\) denote the spline basis vector evaluated at the stand age covariate for a given observation:

\[ \log \mu = \begin{cases} \alpha_\text{cc} + \mathbf{x}^\top \boldsymbol{\beta}_\text{cc} & \text{CC} \\ \alpha_\text{pct} + \mathbf{x}^\top \boldsymbol{\beta}_\text{pct} & \text{PCT} \end{cases} \]

The treatment effect is the ratio of the PCT to no-PCT expected abundance at the same forest age:

\[ \frac{\mu_\text{pct}}{\mu_\text{cc}} = \exp\!\bigl(\alpha_\text{pct} - \alpha_\text{cc} + \mathbf{x}^\top(\boldsymbol{\beta}_\text{pct} - \boldsymbol{\beta}_\text{cc})\bigr) \]

4.3 Priors

Parameter Prior
\(\alpha_\text{cc}\), \(\alpha_\text{pct}\) \(\mathcal{N}(\hat\mu_\text{LiDAR},\; f\,\hat\sigma_\text{LiDAR})\)
\(\boldsymbol{\beta}_\text{cc}\) (K=6) \(\mathcal{N}(\hat{\boldsymbol\mu}_\text{LiDAR},\; f\,\hat{\boldsymbol\sigma}_\text{LiDAR})\)
\(\boldsymbol{\beta}_\text{pct}\) (K=8) \(\mathcal{N}(\hat{\boldsymbol\mu}_\text{LiDAR},\; f\,\hat{\boldsymbol\sigma}_\text{LiDAR})\)
\(\theta_\text{mbw}\) \(\text{Beta}(1, 1)\)

where \(f\) is the inflation factor controlling how tightly the prior constrains the posterior to the LiDAR estimate (see Section 9.2 for a sensitivity analysis over \(f \in \{1, 2, 4, 10\}\); the production model uses \(f = 1\)).

5 Model Fitting

All models were sampled with CmdStanR: 2 chains × 10,000 iterations (5,000 warmup), adapt_delta = 0.95.

5.1 Code (not evaluated — loads pre-computed)

# -- LiDAR-informed model (LM, FIT_RE_MODELS.R) --
mod <- cmdstan_model("production_run_joint_HELP/joint_HELP_model_re.stan")
fit_LM <- mod$sample(data = stan_data_LM, chains = 2, parallel_chains = 2,
                     iter_warmup = 5000, iter_sampling = 5000, adapt_delta = 0.95)
fit_LM$save_object("production_run_joint_HELP/models/joint_fit_re_LM.rds")

# -- Uninformed model (M, same script) --
fit_M <- mod$sample(data = stan_data_M, chains = 2, parallel_chains = 2,
                    iter_warmup = 5000, iter_sampling = 5000, adapt_delta = 0.95)
fit_M$save_object("production_run_joint_HELP/models/joint_fit_re_M.rds")

6 Data Source Comparison

We compare three age-based models and one score-based alternative:

  • L — LiDAR structural prior only; no survey data (baseline)
  • M — weakly informative prior + MBW surveys
  • LM — LiDAR structural prior + MBW surveys
  • S — hurdle-Poisson with ISHM-predicted stand abundance as predictor

Evaluation strategy: For observations in a model’s training set (M, LM), we use PSIS-LOO which penalizes for in-sample overfitting. For the prior-only model L, lppd is computed by drawing from the LiDAR structural posterior with Beta(1,1) hurdle probabilities (fully held-out). Models L, M, and LM are all evaluated on the same N = 2162 observations, so their ELPD values are directly comparable. The score model S uses a different observation set (N = 2,437 observations with a valid ISHM score within 50 m); spatial leave-one-route-out cross-validation (Section 6.3) provides the fairest comparison between score-based and age-based predictions.

Model comparison. Age-based models evaluated on N = 2162 MBW observations with valid TSCC/TSSI; ΔELPD relative to best age-based model. † Score model (S) evaluated on N = 2,437 observations with valid ISHM score; ELPD not directly comparable — SE(ΔELPD) column shows SE(ELPD) for S. Cor (ρ): Spearman correlation between posterior expected counts and observed counts.
Model LiDAR MBW ELPD ΔELPD SE(ΔELPD) Cor (ρ)
M ✓ -1009.7 0 0 0.032
LM ✓ ✓ -1018.9 -9.2 5.1 -0.016
L ✓ -1936.4 -926.7 32.3 0.011
S † score ✓ -1011.2 — 48.2 0.019

The best-performing age-based model was M (ELPD = -1010). The LM model was marginally behind (ΔELPD = -9.2, SE = 5.1), indicating that the LiDAR prior imposes a small but consistent predictive cost relative to the flat prior — the survey data are sufficiently informative that the structural chronosequence adds little. The prior-only model (L, ELPD = -1936) performed far worse, confirming that the survey data substantially update the prior. The score-based model (S), which substitutes ISHM-predicted stand abundance for stand age as its predictor, is evaluated on N = 2,437 observations with a valid score; spatial leave-one-route-out cross-validation (Section 6.3) provides the most direct comparison between S and the age-based models.

M is used as the primary model for all main results in Section 8.

6.2 M vs LM: where does the LiDAR prior help and hurt?

The LiDAR prior (L) encodes expected abundance from the structural chronosequence alone. When added to the survey model (LM), it pulls the posterior toward the prior curve. The overlay below shows exactly where that pull agrees with the survey data and where it diverges — the direct mechanism behind M outperforming LM on LOO.

6.3 Posterior predictive checks and spatial cross-validation

These two diagnostics assess predictive calibration and spatial generalization independently of the ELPD framework.

Posterior predictive checks (PPC)

Five hundred posterior replications are drawn for each model × dataset combination. Four test statistics are compared between observed data and replicates: proportion of zeros P(Y = 0), mean of non-zero counts, 90th percentile, and maximum count.

Posterior predictive check statistics. ⚠ indicates the observed value falls outside the 90% posterior predictive interval.
Model Statistic Observed Rep. median Rep. [5%, 95%] In interval

L P(zero) 0.901 0.486 [0.051, 0.940]
LM 90th pct. 0.000 0.000 [0.000, 1.000]

LM Mean (pos.) 1.477 1.243 [1.187, 1.297] ⚠
LM P(zero) 0.901 0.901 [0.886, 0.914]

M Max 6.000 5.000 [4.000, 8.000]
M Mean (pos.) 1.477 1.460 [1.349, 1.607]
M P(zero) 0.901 0.901 [0.886, 0.915]

S Mean (pos.) 1.477 1.458 [1.346, 1.571]
S P(zero) 0.901 0.902 [0.886, 0.915]

Spatial leave-one-route-out cross-validation

For each model, routes are held out one at a time using PSIS importance sampling. The score model (S) and prior-only model (L) are included for direct comparison; S uses grouped LOO like the survey models, while L uses lppd (fully held out). Bars show mean route-level ELPD ± 1 SE.

8 Main Results

8.1 Abundance chronosequence

8.2 Treatment effect

PCT treatment effect at selected stand ages.
TSSI (yr) TSCC (yr) CC abund PCT abund Ratio 95% CI Evidence
treatment_effect_mult[1] 0 12 1.009 1.48000e-01 7.110000e+01 [0.00, 193.85] ns
treatment_effect_mult[6] 5 17 0.311 8.58000e-01 1.879000e+01 [0.56, 114.61] ns
treatment_effect_mult[11] 10 22 0.263 8.12000e-01 5.450000e+00 [1.05, 21.01] pos **
treatment_effect_mult[16] 15 27 0.346 9.32000e-01 7.460000e+00 [0.78, 38.28] ns
treatment_effect_mult[21] 20 32 0.923 7.67000e-01 3.961000e+01 [0.11, 203.41] ns
treatment_effect_mult[26] 25 37 10.363 1.17820e+01 5.043020e+03 [0.00, 3959.45] ns
treatment_effect_mult[31] 30 42 415.638 5.56948e+10 3.038865e+10 [0.00, 22510693.77] ns

The PCT treatment effect peaks near TSSI = 31 years (TSCC ≈ 43) with a posterior mean ratio of 2.9975368^{12} (95% CI [0.00, 180649848.50]), indicating that thinned stands support approximately 2.9975368^{12}× the BITH abundance of untreated stands at the same forest age.

8.3 Station-level variation

The model includes flat station-level random intercepts (alpha_station ~ Normal(0, sigma_station)) absorbing persistent location-level heterogeneity not explained by forest age or treatment. An initial formulation nested stations within stands (two-level hierarchy), but the stand-level variance component showed severe non-convergence (Rhat = 1.11, ESS = 18; σstation Rhat = 1.58, ESS = 4), indicating the data cannot distinguish between-stand from within-stand variance. The flat structure is better identified and produces comparable results (see models_twolevel_re/ for the archived two-level fits).

Posterior summary for station-level variance component. exp(mean) gives the multiplicative abundance difference associated with a 1-SD shift on the log scale.
variable mean sd q5 q95 rhat ess_bulk Multiplicative scale
sigma_station 0.168 0.126 0.013 0.411 1 3956.85 1.18

Station-level heterogeneity (σstation = 0.17, 95% CI [0.01, 0.41]) captures persistent site-level differences in BITH abundance not explained by forest age or treatment. On the multiplicative scale, a station 1 SD above average supports approximately 1.19× the expected abundance of a typical station at the same forest age, reflecting local variation in habitat quality, microhabitat, or detectability across sites.

9 Prior Sensitivity and Supplementary Models

9.1 Model comparison: survey-only (M) vs LiDAR-informed (LM)

These panels compare the posterior abundance curves recovered by M (weakly informative prior + MBW surveys) and LM (LiDAR structural prior + MBW surveys), illustrating where the prior shifts the posterior relative to what the survey data alone support. Point estimates use the posterior median.

9.2 Prior inflation sensitivity (inf_factor)

The LiDAR posterior SDs are multiplied by inf_factor before being passed to the Stan model as prior standard deviations. A larger factor gives the survey data more freedom to depart from the LiDAR estimate. Because the primary model (M) uses no LiDAR prior, this section asks whether any LiDAR-informed variant (LM at varying inf_factor) can close the predictive gap to M. We assess inf_factor ∈ {1, 2, 4, 10}.

_Sensitivity results pending._
_Sensitivity results pending._
_Sensitivity results pending._
_LOO table not yet computed. Run SENSITIVITY_INF_FACTOR.R LOO step._

10 HELP Dataset: Status and Integration Plan

The High Elevation Landbird Program (HELP) conducted standardized playback surveys at 220 stations across 44 routes in New Brunswick, 2002–2011. This dataset is available and has been pre-processed (coordinate validation, stand-age re-derivation from 2026 NB harvest shapefiles), but is not included in the current analysis for the following reason.

Missing stand covariates. The ISHM-predicted abundance score and the LiDAR-derived elevation covariate both require LiDAR raster coverage at the survey station. Of the 1,101 HELP station-years with a valid focal stand age (TSCC ∈ [0, 80]), 276 observations (25%) spanning 5 stations across 12 routes have no Focal_Predicted_Abundance because their associated stands fall outside the footprint of the 2016 and 2017 NB LiDAR acquisitions (predominantly Ecoregion 3, Fundy coast). The missing values cannot be imputed — they must come from a real raster source.

What is needed. A DEM/LiDAR product covering the missing Fundy-coast stands is required to:

  1. Compute Elevation for the 2,571 unscored stands outside current LiDAR coverage.
  2. Re-run the ISHM scoring pipeline to generate Focal_Predicted_Abundance for all HELP stations.
  3. Re-derive focal stand covariates for HELP using the updated GeoPackage.

Once those rasters are generated and scored, the HELP integration is straightforward: the model structure (hurdle-Poisson, station RE) is already designed to accommodate a second protocol with a separate hurdle parameter, and the joint_HELP_model_re.stan and FIT_RE_MODELS.R scripts retain full joint-model support.

The 90% complete HELP observations (825 / 1,101 station-years with valid covariates) are preserved in D:/CSVs/Ch2/help_evaluation_2026.csv and will be incorporated once the missing covariate coverage is resolved.
